commentBody: Today I learned how to spell BABA GHANOUJ
Isn’t the J tile worth 10?!?

Maybe I’m late to the party and this has been addressed extensively (I’ve been away from the comments for a few days) but I can’t see the Midi puzzles in the NYT Games app. Should I be able to?
If it helps, I’m on the latest version of the app, on iOS, in Australia

A more fun clue-answer pair would be “Something a Möbius strip has only one of”= EDGE.
Saying it lacks an END isn’t saying anything interesting, since lots of shapes and topological entities also have no “end”.

This comment does have an end, and this is it.
I'll let you decide on cause and effect but just sitting here noting "snarf" being in the crossword twice in recent times and the general worldwide breakdown of societal norms and institutions.

Pretty crunchy for a Tuesday.  I have to say when I first spotted the theme I thought we were looking at a fishing angle? But JLO works too.
Wish I could spell BABAGHANOUJ. I have a strong aversion to eggplant.
Hit a bit of a three way Natick with NATE, DANA and NICKIMINAJ. Should have known the latter I suppose but was so sold on DORA or DARA I got convinced it was RICKI. My bad!
Pretty decent grid considering the constraints and more interesting than the Tuesday norm. Well done.
I hope I never see 32 down again.
Fun puzzle with a witty theme.  Very fun to see all those entries with J as the last letter, and the revealer as the bottom most across.  JLO indeed.  More Tuesdays like this please.

Did this puzzle while absolutely JAMMING OUT to "In The Court of the Crimson King" for the first time at 10:00 PM, so that made today's crossword a wee bit more enjoyable. The theme itself was okay. It was interesting at how much long crossword entries ended with J. Overall, not too bad. My favorite entry was 18A, as I use the phrase a lot. Ciao! 🪿

What you get when you try to force a theme on a Tuesday. 

Ghanouj is extremely rare as a spelling, radio DJs haven’t played a 45 in a decade or three, and fresh OJ is still an abbreviation. And a three way Natick for good measure. 

Kudos for still making it work at a Tuesday difficulty level, but it certainly felt a bit contrived.

Nice to see my first initial be the star of the show for a change. I did not have a problem with the puzzle other than waiting for the crosses on BABA GHANOUJ since there weren't enough spaces for the spelling I'm used to seeing.

Thanks, Daniel, and hope you can take advantage of the peace and quiet to come up with some more puzzles for us!

Our wonderful guest columnist, Mr. Ufomadu, teaches today the magic trick of walking away "while solving this entertaining puzzle by Daniel Bodily or when confronting life’s sometimes less amusing puzzles." Do you know who else used to do this in real life? Albert Einstein. When confronted with a problem that he was having difficulty solving, he would go play the violin or go for a walk or go sailing. Many years ago, I read somewhere that he would sometimes drop everything and go to the movie theater (that one seems to be debatable.) Deb Amlen, on the other hand, walks away and has some chocolate. I do, too – have some chocolate, not go sailing. Well, sometimes I just go to sleep. It works! The only reason I didn't have to do this today is because it's been years since I've had to do it on a Tuesday, even a crunchy one. And today was crunchy. And perfect. Thank you, Mr. Bodily. You never disappoint! Signing off, I leave you with Sting and Sister Moon: https://youtu.be/4qF3ZkvUeWk?si=PM6TTrxDa0fDtTyq
I definitely had AOC in there as the revealer for a while and was very confused about the theme. In other news, I had MISO soup as part of my sushi dinner tonight, so that's a fun coincidence

55D.  

Really?

I know it's Tuesday, but c'mon ... really?

Very quick and nicely quirky.  My mind was on other things until I hit the snags and and had to wake up. I especially liked the masks at home, and  pictured a family of raccoons around the dinner table until a cross got me to the ballgame.   The flurry of Js was fun and I started looking forward to those.

Thank you, Daniel, you do a classy Tuesday puzzle.
Something about all those Js in this puzzle made me smile when I was looking at my completed grid. Thanks, Daniel, for a fun solve!

This was tough and chewy - didn't feel like a Tuesday to me at all - and when I hit the revealer at the very end (I'm a dutifully top-down person) and all those columns unexpectedly lit up at once, I lit up with joy.  Fun!

I found a lot of this harder than usual and that's a good thing.

I had no clue there even was a theme; I literally didn't see it coming.  Great stuff.

(Wow, and XWStats only has this one as Average.  So much mileage to vary.)
SCARF and SNARF get me every. dang. time.

I can’t think of many more difficult letters to feature in a puzzle than J, so kudos for that. (Kudos…maybe K? Nah, there’s always Judo. Shut up.)

Even so, I found this tremendously easy for a Tuesday. I may not know the capital of North Macedonia or the proper spelling of the pampered Mediterranean dish, but I got them easily from the crosses.  Maybe a little more J-Lo focus would have helped? A clue about a female donkey (Jenny)? Or a Lego (the Block)? 

Maybe I’m just salty about 32D. Or maybe I just need some 45D to clear my head. I didn’t hate this, I just wasn’t inspired. Have a good morning, everyone.
Does our esteemed columnist really think that the idea of Sol and Luna as siblings comes from "Lady Marmalade"? Great song but it has nothing to  do with this clue.
Does anyone really use ESPY in conversation, aside from discussing crossword puzzles?

Finished the puzzle except for SNARF. Checked the Google ngram viewer for "snarf down" (doesn't exist) and the Google trends comparison to "scarf down" (not even close in popularity with "scarf down" being the winner). Who says this word... I should've been more into yoga

Daniel chose my other favorite letter for his clever puzzle. Q is still my fave, but J is obviously a close second 😉 I knew BABA GHANOUJ was the answer to 17 down, but I never knew it could end in J 🤯 Our favorite restaurant in Wisconsin served some that was so good. Even my young kids (at the time) ate it! I really liked this theme and the execution was flawless. Well done, Daniel.
Did it occur to anyone else that 23A could have had the same answer if the clue had been “Something I can’t stand for”?
Good work giving our friend the 'j' more gameplay than usual! Impressive

From Wikipedia:

"While the ingredients vary from region to region, the essentials (eggplants, tahini, garlic, lemon) are generally the same."

So, BABAGHANOUJ is very similar to hummus. Just substitute eggplant for the chickpeas in hummus and you've got it. Spices will vary maybe.

One thing that ticked me off in today's otherwise enjoyable puzzle was the clue for 71 across. 
This is not packaged in sticks. We are not in a looney tunes cartoon, while the clue can be decoded, it's sub par to me, NYT clues may exploit stereotypes but should not perpetuate misconceptions.
I was literally driving through the capital of North Macedonia when I got that clue!!
Nobody spells baba ganoush that way !

I found myself saying "I DUNNO" a few times.  

That's rare on a Tuesday.

Made for a fun solve.

Even on a Tuesday, Mr. Bodily's deft hand is unmistakable.

Good morning for the trees, with ASH and OAK, and the cereal grains, with OAT, RYE, and also ORZO, which is the Italian word for "barley." (Do Italian chefs get confused when a recipe says "add 100g orzo"?)

(Michael Pollen once commented that the entire history of plant life on Earth was an epic battle between the trees and the grasses; with the grasses currently in the lead.)

Rutherford Platt's *A Pocket Guide to the Trees* (1953) is perhaps the most lyrical field guide ever penned. From the entry on "White Ash":

"The parts of ash are so finely wrought that, separated from the tree, each is a thing of beauty in itself. Put together, these artistic details build a tree that wins first prize in architecture. The trunk is utterly simple, lifting the tree upward out of the ground with the harmony of a temple column . . .  In fact, ash wood is so tough and elastic that it makes the best paddles, as well as tennis rackets, hoe and rake handles, bows and arrows. Ash wood burns while still green because sap is inflammable."

Reading this, why would you want to?

Got the theme fairly early, which helped me spell BABAGHANOUJ in the manner intended (which to me is decidedly a variant and should have been clued as such). When the reveal arrived at 72A I felt a bit underwhelmed, though it was certainly apt.

Took me a bit longer than usual for a Tuesday, in part because of my spelling woes and in part due to lots of typos that I needed to locate and fix before filling my last box.

Decent enough theme, though JTILE/RADIODJ felt rather forced to me.

I don't get the dislikes here. It took me a while, but "answers ending in J" is a perfectly clever theme, especially when coupled with the 72A revealer. By coincidence, at our book group meeting tonight (51 years and still going strong!) we're reading Burma Sahib by Paul Theroux, a novel based on George Orwell's time in the service of the BRITISHRAJ.

When my son was at the University of Montana he played in an alt-bluegrass band called Baba Ganoush, so that had me for a little while. It's hard to think of 58A without a fond memory of Molly Ivins, whose belittling sobriquet for George W. Bush was SHRUB.

Fun Tuesday! Thanks!
This puzzle might as well have been written in Macedonian.

So while I much enjoyed this puzzle last night (see earlier comment below) I see the NYT still hasn't fixed the major bugs in the web site.  They've removed the non-functioning Admire button from the solved screen, but since I went back to review answers that posters were referencing only by coordinate (and please stop doing that) the site now tells me I spent 7 hours 20 minutes on this puzzle when I flip the timer on.  Worse than useless.  How do people who value their completion stats even tolerate this brokenness?

Wow, that's tough for a Tuesday puzzle.
This one really did some Bodily harm to my average time.

Nice Tuesday puzzle!

I like a puzzle that shows a little love for an under-appreciated letter.  So, hats off and PJ's down low for the letter J.

However, I can't resist including a nit:
When do you suppose the last time a RADIO DJ spun an actual "45"?
My guess would be 1982.

Since the revealer was in it's proper place the thought crossed, "Boy, a lot of J's".  Had fun with this one, thanks. 

Alas, due to the Emerald Ash Borer the US East Coast misses that species.

I liked this, decent general set of answers even with 6 themed ones.
Have to admit I was a touch worried about the amount of gaps after my first run through the clues.
Correct me if im wrong but "Let's go Muchachos"  =  "vamanos", the plural form, not "vamos" which is 2nd person singular.  Who am into get in the way of a crossword constructor though.

I sCarfed down my breakfast this morning, but, apparently, I should have SNARFed it down . . .

Happy Tuesday, everyone -- an warmer treat of a day in these parts today!😎
Surely @Andrzej found this theme delightful?
Doh! So her nickname isn't pronounced "Jello"?

I love Baba Ganoush 
Baba Ghanoush
Baba Ghanouj
Baba Gannuj
Baba Ghanoug
Baba Ghannouj
Baba Ganouj
but I can't figure out how to spell it!
Thank you for a FUN puzzle!

Oh, nice one, Mr. Bodily! 

I spent this entire delightful solve intrigued the high number of J's, *without*  twigging to the J-Lo(w) theme 🙄. Heading off to use power tools now, I'm sure it'll work out just fine...

I’ve always felt an affinity for the letter “J,” given my name…and of my great grandfather, my grandfather, my father, my eldest son, and his son…so I am a fan of this puzzle, which I finished around 20% under my average time. I did get hung up a tad on 17D, BABA GHANOUJ; although I knew what the clue was asking for, I had no idea until today it could be spelled with “J” at the end.

I have loved the name of this dish ever since I was a kid and heard Hans Conried say it on an episode of Make Room for Daddy sometime more than sixty years ago…and whenever I think of the word, I hear it in his distinctive voice.
What fun!
So much fun doing the puzzle and reading this!

So many "J"s. That I noticed. But did I notice that they were all at the bottom of long Down entries?  Of course not.

So I was not expecting JLO and I let out a small chuckle when I saw her.  Another very cute and nicely executed early week puzzle -- the second in a row.  Does this portend good things for the entire week?  Heaven forbid I speak too soon.  We shall see.

BTW, I made no attempt to spell BABAGHANOUJ once I knew that would be the answer.  I let it come in on its own, which turned out to have been a wise decision.  I would have ended it with an SH because that's how I say it. And who knows how many other spelling mistakes I would have also made?

A breezy, enjoyable Tuesday with a most original theme and a great revealer.

Fun puzzle and a good workout. With my wife's common nicknames being "Doctor J" and one of our favorite dogs - "OJ" this should have been right up my alley. But just a lot of working the crosses and it all came together. Somewhat appropriate puzzle find today was a Thursday from November 25, 2010 by Bill Thompson. Some theme answers in that one: MALCOLMX LLCOOLJ SPECIALK GENERATIONZ AVENUEQ Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=11/25/2010&g=27&d=D Might put another puzzle find in a reply. ...

Having just watched Amazon Prime’s excellent 4-part series Soul Power on the American Basketball Association, I initially had 72A as DrJ (though it turns out he was actually born in East Meadow, NY). Guess that would’ve been too much of a slam DUNK.

A fabulous puzzle focused on a letter that made a very late appearance to our current alphabet!

In fact, “j” was simply considered a long “i” for most of its history and not a letter in its own right. Folks would most commonly add a bit of a flair to the final “i” when writing out Roman numerals to make the value easier to distinguish - think _“viii”_ vs _“viij”_

Anyways, this state of affairs was around until the 19th century, which is why DC has no J street (straight from I to K we go!)

All right, taking my Fact Boy hat off for the day.

Having never hear of our columnist for the past two days, Ikechukwu Ufomadu, I strolled over to YouTube and watched a few of his videos.  His comedy is an acquired taste to be sure, but he had me giggling.  I recommend taking a gander.
Delightful puzzle today! Thank you so much!
Gitchie, gitchie, ya-ya, da-da.  Thanks for the morning earworm, Ikechukwu!  I also went awry with EXTRAS, wound up just below my average time.  And thanks Daniel, for the excellent Tuesday!

Anyone else have IOwA as something I can stand for? I was thinking of the word IOTA meaning "tiny amount," rather than the Greek character. I don't watch a lot of TV, so wBS sounded like a plausible TV network. Took a while for me to sort that out, but the streak lives on!

An easy puzzle until it wasn’t. Sometimes there’s just stuff you don’t know, but it’s the constructors job to guide the solver through the smoke with adept cluing, which was sadly absent here. Not a very fun puzzle unfortunately. And hey @NYT, let’s please not include right-wing grifters who turn on their supporters for personal gain, how about that?

Awesome puzzle, loved the letter variety (in fact I thought it was a pangram right up until the very end!)

Trickiest answer for me was "Snarf", as being an Irish person (and not very interested in yoga) the first thought I had was "Scarf", as was the case in quite a few recent puzzles.

I've made a scant few crosswords myself, mostly for fun, and sometimes I include the phrase ', but not that one' as an indicator to help smooth out ambiguity in tough clues crossed with names. I don't know if that's a setting faux pas, but I like the idea for rare words and phrases. (For example, cluing "Roti" as 'Indian flatbread, but not that one')
While it’s true that a Mobius strip has no end, that’s true of any old loop, circle, or sphere. A Mobius strip is more fun than that—it has only one side and one edge.
Sucks not knowing how to spell babagonuj
UGH!!! Twice my normal solve time for a Tuesday! Really Daniel?
Enjoyed this one.  Fun revealer.

I don’t watch a lot of basketball, baseball, or football. Is it common for the players to have to stop mid-play and tie their shoes, because they forgot? (I know in ‘soccer’ they’re always tripping over their laces and hurting themselves.)
I just love the construction of this puzzle, from the unusual theme requirement all the way down to placing the theme clue in the most appropriate place possible.
I spent 10% of my solving time on the main part of the puzzle, and 90% trying to spell "babaghannouuouszh," which, as it turns out, is not the correct spelling.
I would not have known the particular spelling of GHANOUJ were it not for the Rutgers grease trucks!

What a fun puzzle, and for me it was crunchy for a Tuesday. It took me longer than it should have to see what was going on with the letter J, and the spelling of Babaghanouj was tricky too. I used to play a lot of Scrabble with my husband and kids, but that was years ago, so it didn't help me much.
Thanks to Mr. Bodily and the whole team for a great start to my day.

ASANA crossed with SNARF (when SCARF is better known)
DANA crossed with NATE (granted, A is the only thing that could work)

Obscure in both directions requires guesswork.
This made me smile. Nice distraction from my overcrowded train.

SCARF/SNARF is fast-becoming a kealoa for me. Must remember not to fill it in too quickly, or else I'll doom myself to hunting down one incorrect letter when the puzzle is finished (like I did today). 

I really liked the clue for 23A.

One more answer history search, inspired by this puzzle's theme. Wondered how many answers there might be that have 3 (or more) J's in them. Well.... 

...in the history of these puzzles, exactly two. 

DJJAZZYJEFF
and
JJONAHJAMESON

And that's it. 

....

Turns out Raleigh and Mariner have the same number of letters 😂 that slowed down the NW for me, but got me excited for the season! 
I thought this was a bit crunchy for a Tuesday, and I'm not complaining one bit. Lots of fun crosses, like SING with ARIA, and UMP with MARINER. And the translation of BABA GHANOUJ made my day!

I was taken aback (not in a good way) by the clue for 20 across; here in NZ referring to Māori as “native” would be considered extremely old fashioned at best, but more likely quite offensive. For quite a while I had the word “kauri” in there, referring to one of our most magnificent native trees, although I knew really it was unlike to be that. 

Here the term “native” is associated with flora and fauna rather than people. It’s disappointing that no one took the two minutes to search this and amend it - even adding “native peoples”would have been much better.

Constructors have a lot of license with some words. 

There could be a new rendition of Let's Call The Whole Thing Off that would go something like:

"You spell Khadafy and I spell Gaddafi"
"You spell hummus and I spell hoummos"

Then you get to "babaganoush", and things really start to open up.

Hey Rich,
Speaking of 21Down, don't I recall you have one on your PH?

Okay - one more puzzle find. One of the most unusual I've ever encountered. A Sunday from December 31, 1972 by Frances Hansen with the title: "Time Remembered" Five 23 letter grid-spanning theme answers in that one. TELLMETELLMEISITTRUENOT HINGSLEFTOFSEVENTYTWOTI MESATHIEFWEOUGHTTOBANWO ULDITSPOILSOMEAWESOMEPL ANIFIWROTEMYCONGRESSMAN Whew. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=12/31/1972&g=45&d=A ...

Today’s poem made from words found in today’s puzzle

night serenade

a/ as the tree burns
i stand as close as the wolf
d/ and something is shaped
in the sound
a/ i sing

Constructing a crossword puzzle requires skill, imagination and smarts; submitting the puzzle to the NY Times requires patience and optimism; publication of the puzzle requires a willingness to suffer the slings and arrows of snarky solvers, who will pick it apart, denigrate your intelligence, and make cracks ranging from nitty to insulting.  

To the puzzle makers, who have the passion for crosswords that gives them the courage to go through all of this, THANK YOU.  We depend on you to make puzzles for us, so please keep up the good work.  

To the hopeful creators who are trying their best to make good puzzles and see them in print, be brave. The critics take a lot for granted, but they will secretly love you too (or just pretend they will, despite all evidence to the contrary).

Oh well I finally got around to last Saturday’s! Had had a lot of company, sort of.

Anyway it was a great Saturday. Haven’t read all the comments yet but well
my year has suddenly gotten busy and I’m a little distracted. One of the funniest to me was I had GYM for “a place to sweat” crossing “Like the climax of Bolero” and kept thinking “nO timpani” but I know it has a lot of Timpani and didn’t even think of using Italian! Will keep that in mind for future music clues. 

I got most of the bottom and most of the middle(was like “Who was IONE?” Until reading the notes): CARD-SHARK from Caitlin really helped. But still had a lot of
Word-check to do before finishing this one! Many of the words I had were right but some like GYM were not. ‘Make up of a plot” also got me: LAND. Deceptively simple. 
I had thought of ACRE for that one, but no.

I know that it isn’t that hard for the editors to turn up the dial on difficulty if enough people complain so I don’t usually comment on difficulty but yes this was more like 2020.  And it was very good! Liked the grid design, too. Thanks!
That was very hard for me for a Tuesday. I enjoyed it, another great learning puzzle.
I've only ever heard of "scarfing" something down, so SNARF repeatedly trips me up even though I know it's a term.
I have a number of complaints about this puzzle (TNT doesn't come in sticks). This puzzle was made more difficult by questionable clues and alternative spellings.
I know difficulty standards are in the gutter now. But "51-Down without its first letter" is egregiously bad, even for the modern crossword

Interesting puzzle idea. First time I've ever seen that spelling for that particular Mediterranean dish. Would rather have seen LLCOOLJ or HEYMRDJ instead of the rapper with unfortunate political allies, but hey, that's the world we live in now.
Good Tuesday! What Ikechukwu Ufomadu said is right that going away and coming back does help. I don’t know whether it would have helped with last Saturday’s.

I object to  the cross NATE/JTILE, especially on a Tuesday!  The former is a name of a rather unknown person and the latter I thought would be a single word.

⭐️⭐️
I had no idea that babaganoush, which I ate many times while in Iran in the 1970s, was considered a Mediterranean dish.
I’m pretty sure ScARF for SCARF wronged me sometime in the past, but I didn’t learn.
I have never in my life seen that dish spelled like that, ruined my solve trying to work out where I had gone wrong. Oh well :(
was scratching my head about iota... jlo was a necessary clue to get to the expected spelling of babaganoush.

Really fun puzzle. Started with all the acrosses and was concerned about what long down would end with a J...then the final Js kept coming and I realized it had to be a theme! Thanks for keeping us on our toes.
Iota? Someone please explain!
I got JLO before the themed clues. Then the lightbulb went off. Fun puzzle today!
@Mike.  Me too

@Mike

Well, you learned one way to spell it.

@Steve L
But not the right way, IMO.

@Rich Roberts

No.

@Rich Roberts

This one confused me too because J’s are worth 10 on Words With Friends, which is my only experience with Scrabble. Maybe the classic board game has different point values?

@Valerie
That's like saying that I can't understand why my heart didn't take the trick in Bridge. It always works in Hearts, which is my only experience with Bridge.

@SBK

Wow!  That’s exactly what it’s like. You nailed it!🙄😂

@Alexis
I vaguely recall someone mentioning that it might not be available on that platform outside the US.  For what it’s worth, I’m on the latest iOS, using the latest version of the NYT Games app.  The midi is there, along with its own archives.

@Madangmom

Yeah that's not right, I have the Midi here in Singapore...

@Alexis,

There have been lots of complaints lately in the Comments about the MIDI not showing up in the Games app. Doesn’t seem to be related to where you are in the world. I don’t play it, but some have said that refreshing the screen helps.

@NYC Traveler

I've tried different ways to find the Midi:

Restarting the computer
Googling "nyt midi today"

What worked today was: after doing the Mini, the MIdi finally showed up

I have another issue sometimes: the website won't let me look at the completed puzzle--and today was no lookups!!!

@Cat Lady Margaret

I think it has two edges but only one side.

@Cat Lady Margaret

Yes exactly, any old loop has no end - as you say the twist (ha!) with a möbius strip is that has only one side (or edge)

@Cat Lady Margaret 

I think it simply lacks an end as opposed to a normal STRIP. 

Also, a Möbius strip is often metaphorically used for the concept of infinity; in other words, it has no END. 

One EDGE doesn't have quite the philosophical appeal.
@Colin A strip joined end-to-end without a twist — an ordinary ring — also has no end. What distinguishes a Mobius strip is that it only has one side, and one edge.

@Cat Lady Margaret - I considered this to be a gentle form of misdirection, since everybody knows the Möbius strip has only one edge and side. 

I suspect most people didn't immediately think of "end" when they saw the clue.

@SP

I enjoyed this puzzle too - found it fun  with the J endings.   I also was dismayed by the triple trivia name challenge!
Yes, could be resolved by JTILE, but like our columnist, I tried to make a scrabble word out of "J--LE" which I  filled in "JouLE" (lol!).
This is my 2nd  recent weird stutter on "Tile" related to Scrabble.  I think because when I play (not often due to usually getting trounced!), I use an app, so no handling of actual tiles.  I've come to think of them as just letters -no JTILE .

@Gregg
As an answer or as a person?
@SBK I’m assuming he means for her bizarre political turn.
@Gregg in my head her first name is now Tricky

@Rachel yep the crossings made it work out but my initial fill had "retro DJ" and "babaghanous" instead. "J tile" was easy to fill in the end but my mind was already calculating potential 8 point words instead and I was mildly disappointed. That said, still a normal satisfying solve, just with a slightly bitter aftertaste!
@Rachel - "Hey, Mr DJ, when you gonna spin it?"  Fun puzzle!
@Rachel Community radio DJs probably play more vinyl now than they did a few decades ago.
@Rachel Strong agree on all. A triple Natick? C’mon.

@sotto voce


And Thomas Edison used to go fishing off his pier at his Fort Myers, Florida, home when he was stuck on a problem -- because no one bothers a person who is fishing, and he could let his mind wander.  They say that he wouldn't bait his hook, either, because he didn't want to be pestered by the fish, either.
@Grumpy - Agree 55D is a lame clue.  I don't think the constructor should prophet from such entries.

@Grumpy

It's a name crossed with two names, and one of those cross is extremely difficult. The linked clue adds rigidness to the solve, I like the choice, personally.

@Grumpy

Yes, and 43A and 66D are…debatable.

Tiff,
Yoga will help you resolve that.

@Tiff No matter how often I see it in the crossword, I refuse to believe in the word snarf.  I categorically deny it entry into my personal lexicon.  If I ever hear someone say it in real life, it will probably precipitate my demise.

@SBK
Uh, no, I believe it's word play. Reading the clue for 11D reminded our columnist of Lady Marmalade. Who doesn't hear that song now when reading "Sol's sister"?

@Nora

Not to be confused with Sister Souljah which would have been a great across entry!

@Katie
Thanks. Dare I ask your cat's name and what you serenade her with?

@Joe
It would be pretty easy to come up with a list of a few dozen words that are only seen in crossword puzzles.

@Joe 

Sure. You have to lean forward, shade your eyes with one hand, and place the other hand on your hip when you use it, though.

@Joe
Only when discussing sports awards.

@Snarf disliker

It’s a slang word for eating quickly and greedily. There are sandwich shops here called Snarf’s.

@Snarf disliker

I too balked at this the first time it came up, but it is quite a frequent answer, much more so than scarf. You might need to snarf down a few more crosswords before you get used to it though.
@Menc Maybe it’s a regional term. Whereabouts are you located? Here in the northeast US it’s not a term I’ve ever heard of before seeing it in the puzzle.
@LG I now see that locations are shown lol

@Snarf disliker We always said “snarf” when you laugh hard with food in your mouth and you spray it out your mouth and some of it goes up your nose. That last part is especially key. Good times. And “scarf” for eating quickly.
@Snarf disliker snarf was my mistake. I had scarf and it took me a bit to go back to the yoga down clue which I had filled in with the across.
@MB this is the correct definition of SNARF.
@Menc Our favorite sandwich shop!

@Snarf disliker

@lucky13

@Rob Doughty

@Rob Doughty

I do admire your confidence.

Second person singular is "ve."  It is more commonly used reflexively, as "vete."  As in "Vete a la bodega," or "Go to the corner store."

"Vamos" can mean "we are going" or "let's go," and intonation and context tell you which meaning is meant.  "Vamos a la bodega a la una; ¿necesitas algo?" means "We're going to the store at 1:00; do you need anything?" while "Tengo una idea, vamos a la bodega" means "I've got an idea, let's go to the store."

"Vámonos" is the reflexive form, and is used for emphasis:  "Vámonos muchacho; ya es tarde" means, "Let's go, boy, it's already late."

"Vamanos" is not a thing.

Former Spanish, ESL and bilingual teacher here.  Hope this helps!

@Rebecca
It's funny, our use of terms. Thus we have "Native" Americans, "Indigenous" Canadians*, and "Aboriginal" Australians, even though those three words mean roughly the same thing. 

And on one hand, having been born in the USA (Ohio, to be specific), I'm as "native" an American as any; and on the other, our peoples all came and settled from somewhere else, even if it was over the Bering land bridge, or the Coral Sea.

*The current term in Canada is "First Nations," which I rather like:  even if it isn't entirely accurate--there were probably nations before theirs--it acknowledges that those peoples had polities equally valid as those of the French and English, etc.

@Jeff

I assume you're new at crosswords.  ASANA is not an obscure word if you know the least thing about yoga, which is an activity that I've never participated in.  However, even if you confuse yoga with Yoda, the word has appeared 92 times before in the NYT, including once this month, twice this year, five times last year, and seven times the year before.  In fact, today's clue is one of the most basic and common ways to clue it.  (12 appearances).

If you're new, you should commit words like ASANA to memory, because with their common letters, they will appear over and over.  

If you're not new, I'm amazed you didn't know it.

@Thomas the clue [Something I can stand for?] is written in the first person from the point of view of Iota Jones, the world renown 18th century virtuoso and raconteur.  The “I,” therefore, is referring to himself as his first initial. Just as “A” might stand for myself, Ace.  With that understanding the clue is a simple one. This trick however, is guessing which of the approximately 115 billion humans who have lived and died on Earth since the dawn of the modern homo sapien the “I” is referring to. One might argue that it’s a bit of a long shot to guess one in 115 billion correctly, but in my case, I got it on the first try brah.
